title: run-work-order-loop has no alternative entry point for substrates that legitimately prune the work-orders block kind
description: FGAP-151 (companion) documents the immediate fix path — surface the work-orders-block-kind dependency in the tool description + install ceremony so substrate authors do not encounter it by surprise. This FGAP captures the separate scope-decision: whether to add an alternative entry point (e.g. a --inline work-order JSON parameter to run-work-order-loop, or a sibling tool run-inline-work-order accepting one work-order record directly) for substrates that legitimately prune work-orders. Discovered by clock-menu-app usage-feedback FB-014 (2026-05-29T13:37Z): the user's substrate setup is minimal-by-intent (10 standard block kinds + 1 custom usage-feedback kind, work-orders pruned); under FGAP-151 documentation alone, the user knows what they are losing but still has no path to invoke the orchestrator iteration semantics (attestation + human-OK gate per DEC-0047) without re-installing the block kind. The user-hit-it dimension persists past the documentation fix.

evidence.reference: work-order-loop core implementation is fully parameterized over a single WorkOrderRecord — the iteration mechanics + attestation pipeline + human-OK gate do not structurally require the record to come from a block read. The block-read is a single layer at the entry point.

Tool surface accepts a work_order_id parameter; substituting (or augmenting with) a work_order parameter accepting an inline WorkOrderRecord JSON would expose the same iteration mechanics without the block-kind prerequisite.

FB-014 verbatim: substrate pruned work-orders intentionally; tool became inaccessible; no workaround. Expected-behavior section names exactly this alternative: 'Or work orders could be stored in a different block or passed inline.'

Substrate-surfaces investigation report verdict separating documentation-fix (FGAP-151 scope) from alternative-entry-point gap (this FGAP scope). Per feedback_intentional_doesnt_close_gap.md: by-design verdict on coupling does not close the user-hit-it gap on alternative entry.

impact: Substrates that minimize installed block kinds (project-local micro-substrates; demo configurations; learning examples; substrates with non-orchestrator shape) have no path to invoke the run-work-order-loop iteration + attestation + human-OK gate semantics without re-installing work-orders. The tool's design pattern (canonical iteration semantics) is valuable independent of the block-storage choice, but the only entry point ties it to block storage. Result: a usable iteration primitive is gated behind a substrate-shape choice that may be unrelated to the iteration use case.
canonical_vocabulary: alternative entry point; tool variant; inline-input parameter
proposed_resolution: Scope-decision required from user (operator decides whether to build): (a) add --inline / --work-order JSON parameter to run-work-order-loop accepting a WorkOrderRecord directly, bypassing the readBlock call when present; (b) add sibling Pi tool run-inline-work-order accepting the same record type without a loop wrapper; (c) leave as-is and rely on FGAP-151 documentation to clarify install-time choice. Implementation cost (a) is low — the work-order-loop core function is already parameterized over WorkOrderRecord; entry point would dispatch to either readBlock or the inline param. Trade-off: option (a) increases the tool surface (more parameters; precedence rule between work_order_id and work_order); option (c) keeps the surface minimal at the cost of usability cliff for pruned substrates.
